The idea that making a voluntary commitment makes people much more susceptible to persuasion attempts is illustrated by one study of pain handlers in France. The researchers approached people on the street and asked some of them, Sorry, would you have some coins for me to take the bus, please? This request got about 10% of people to agree to give them money, on average the equivalent of 48 cents. But the experimenters also approached others on the street and made the same request while adding, but of course you are free to accept or refuse. Simply adding this reminder that their actions were voluntary resulted in a 400% increase in the number of people who gave money and more than double the average amount given to the equivalent of a dollar and four cents. In other words, when donors were reminded that their donation decision was voluntary, they were more likely to make a voluntary commitment. Now, this wasn't the only demonstration of the effectiveness of this small phrasing. In a set of 42 separate experiments, adding to a request the words, but of course you are free to decline or a similar phrase such as, of course you're free to do as you wish, significantly increased compliance. Moreover, this was the case for a wide variety of requests, for example, making contributions to a charity, participating in an unpaid survey, and purchasing food from a door-to-door solicitor. So, when you are asking for an initial agreement, employing this kind of phrase is certainly something to consider.
